healthMetrics.cigMethodologyNote: Berkeley Earth conversion: 22.0 µg/m³ PM2.5 ≈ 1 cigarette/day. This compares population-level mortality risk, not individual clinical outcomes. Acute vs chronic exposure differs significantly.
healthMetrics.aqliMethodologyNote: AQLI methodology: each 10 µg/m³ PM2.5 above WHO baseline (5.0 µg/m³) ≈ 0.98 years of life expectancy lost. Calculated from annual mean PM2.5 (long-term exposure), not annual median.

seasonalEvents.cause: Diwali pyrotechnics combined with Punjab/Haryana post-harvest stubble burning peak simultaneously
Cold air mass + low wind traps vehicle, industrial and biomass burning emissions in the Indo-Gangetic Plain
Southwest monsoon suppresses dust and washes particulates
Westerly winds carry Rajasthan desert dust

methodology.cigaretteEquivalence: Berkeley Earth conversion: 22.0 µg/m³ PM2.5 ≈ 1 cigarette/day. This compares population-level mortality risk, not individual clinical outcomes. Acute vs chronic exposure differs significantly.
methodology.aqli: AQLI methodology: each 10 µg/m³ PM2.5 above WHO baseline (5.0 µg/m³) ≈ 0.98 years of life expectancy lost. Calculated from annual mean PM2.5 (long-term exposure), not annual median.
methodology.dataSource: OpenAQ API v3 /sensors/{id}/days pre-computed daily aggregates

section3.title: The Winter Crisis
section3.narrative: Each autumn, three forces converge over the Indo-Gangetic Plain simultaneously. Farmers in Punjab and Haryana burn millions of acres of post-harvest crop stubble. Diwali fireworks add a concentrated particulate spike. Then winter cold settles in: low wind speeds, temperature inversions, a meteorological lid that traps Delhi's own vehicle exhaust and industrial emissions. November median in our dataset: 250.6 µg/m³. Not elevated—catastrophic. Every single day in November recorded Hazardous PM2.5. The cigarette equivalence on a median November day is 11 cigarettes. December and January are nearly as severe at 216 and 215 µg/m³.

section5.narrative: Delhi's seasonal range is the widest in our dataset. August median—the city's best month—is 37 µg/m³: already Unhealthy for Sensitive Groups by WHO standards. November median is 250.6 µg/m³. The gap is 6.7×. In any other city we track, August-level air would be cause for concern. In Delhi, it is the breathing window. The critical insight: Delhi has no months that meet WHO PM2.5 guidelines. The monsoon offers relative relief—not clean air.

section6.title: Your Breathing Window
section6.intro: Delhi has no months that meet WHO PM2.5 guidelines (15 µg/m³ annual target). The goal here is harm reduction: finding the months where air is merely bad rather than crisis-level.

bestWindow.detail: Southwest monsoon suppresses dust and washes particulates. August is the best month at 37 µg/m³ median—still Unhealthy for Sensitive Groups, but the only window where outdoor activity is broadly feasible. N95 masks still recommended for sensitive groups and extended exercise.

caution.detail: Pre-monsoon season brings westerly winds carrying Rajasthan desert dust. March–May medians range 52–62 µg/m³: consistently Unhealthy. September marks the monsoon exit: air worsens week by week as October approaches. Limit outdoor time and mask up for exercise.

highRisk.detail: The crisis season. October marks rapid onset: median jumps from 41 µg/m³ in September to 105 µg/m³. November is catastrophic at 250 µg/m³. Avoid all non-essential outdoor exposure. Air purifiers are essential indoors. Consider postponing travel during these months if you have any respiratory or cardiovascular conditions.
